coredata.dc:title: Preparation and application of silver nanoparticle capped carbon dots composite as fluorescent probe for cobalt determination in vitamin B12 supplement samples
coredata.dc:creator: Seedad, Ratchadaporn
Prayongpan, Pornpimol
Chualong, Kamonchanok
Karuwan, Chanpen
Songsrirote, Kriangsak
coredata.dc:description: A simple and economical material of fluorescence nanocomposite was developed to quantify vitamin B12 through cobalt(II) ion measurement. The material was derived from carbon dots capped with silver nanoparticles, forming spherical AgNPs@CDs composite with the average diameter of 8.7 nm, expressing unique optical properties, and was used as an optical probe for Co(II) ion determination. The synthesized AgNPs@CDs showed a maximum absorption peak at the wavelength of 406 nm, while their fluorescence peak appeared at the wavelength of 526 nm under the excitation wavelength of 406 nm. The nanocomposites exhibited stable fluorescence with IC50 value against human cholangiocyte cell line of 137.7 ppm after 24 h of the treatment. The interaction between Co(II) ions and AgNPs@CDs effervesced molecular oxygen and caused fluorescence quenching of the composite material. The optimum conditions for Co(II) ion detection were pH 10, volume ratio between AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ion of 0.10:0.90, and reaction time of 30 min. The linearity range based on the Stern–Volmer plot was 0.05–10.00 ppm, and the limit of detection and limit of quantification were 0.032 ppm and 0.095 ppm, respectively. The developed approach was used to determine the extracted Co(II) ions in commercial vitamin B12 supplement samples and their spiked extracts. The results showed good agreement with those from a conventional method of atomic emission spectrometry, and acceptable range of recovery from 90.0 to 105.0 % was obtained. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the AgNPs@CDs was elucidated based on the characterization information from several analytical techniques, and electrocatalytic reaction between the nanocomposite and Co(II) species was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Fluorescent probe
Vitamin B12
Metal detection
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the AgNPs@CDs and investigation on reaction mechanism
Optimum conditions and analytical performance for cobalt(II) ion detection using the AgNPs@CDs
Extraction procedure of cobalt(II) ions from supplement samples
Characterization of optical and physical properties of the synthesized AgNPs@CDs
Optimal conditions for Cobalt(II) ion determination using AgNPs@CDs
Fluorescent stability of the prepared AgNPs@CDs
Toxicity of the AgNPs@CDs to human cell line
Performance of AgNPs@CDs fluorescence on Cobalt(II) ion determination
Determination of cobalt(II) ions in commercial nutritional supplement samples and method validation
Mechanism of interaction between AgNPs@CDs and cobalt(II) ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by Srinakharinwirot University (Grant No.412/2565), and I would like to thank the SLRI for the allocation of synchrotron radiation beamtime. I would like to thank Dr. Wutthikrai Busayaporn for assistant in XAS conceptual discussions. Assistance provided from beamline 8 (BL8) staff members of the synchrotron facility is also gratefully acknowledged. In addition, I would like to thank Assoc. Prof. Arthit Chairoungdua from Department of Physiology, Faculty of Science, Mahidol University and Assist. Prof. Nuttapon Apiratikul from Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science , Srinakharinwirot University for their kind support on cytotoxic experiment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption (Abs) and fluorescence (Flu) spectra, and (B) the fluorescence decay curves of the AgNPs@CDs and CDs.
TEM image of the (A) CDs and (B) AgNPs@CDs with the HR-TEM image (inset).

Level of fluorescence quenching of AgNPs@CDs in the presence of 10 ppm Co(II) ions with (A) different pH values (3–12) at the volume ratio of 0.50:0.50 and reaction time of 30 min, (B) different volume ratios at pH 10 and reaction time of 30 min, and (C) different reaction times at pH 10 and volume ratio of 0.10:0.90.

Normalized fluorescence intensity of the AgNPs@CDs as a function of (A) irradiation time by Xe lamp continuously for 120 min, (B) NaCl concentration ranging from 0.0 to 1.0 M, (C) temperature ranging from 27 °C to 100 °C, and storage time 4 °C and ambient temperature.
Cell viability of MMNK-1 cell from the cytotoxicity analysis of AgNPs@CDs for 24 h.

(A) Relative percent of fluorescence quenching of AgNPs@CDs in the presence of 10 ppm of different metal ions, anions, and some bioactive molecules compared to Co(II) ions under optimum conditions. (B) Fluorescence spectra of AgNPs@CDs with different Co(II) ion concentrations, and calibration curve of Co(II) ion quantification using AgNPs@CDs as detection sensor (onset). (C) Mixture solutions between AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ions at different concentrations.

XPS spectrum of MSA-CdTe QDs (A), and deconvoluted XPS peaks of (B) O 1s and (C) N 1s of AgNPs@CDs, and (D) O 1s and (E) N 1s of AgNPs@CDs in the presence of Co(II) ions. (F) XANES spectra of Co K-edge of Co(II) ions with AgNPs@CDs and Co(ClO4)2·6H2O.
Schematic illustration of mechanisms for molecular oxygen evolution [55] and fluorescence quenching of AgNPs@CDs by oxygen.
Comparison of the performance of the fluorescent AgNPs@CDs as an alternative method for the detection of cobalt ions to assess the quantity of vitamin B12.
Concentrations and recoveries of cobalt(II) ions determined in commercial vitamin B12 containing supplement samples using the fluorescent AgNPs@CDs analysis and ICP-OES (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Preparation and application of silver nanoparticle capped carbon dots composite as fluorescent probe for cobalt determination in vitamin B12 supplement samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and economical material of fluorescence nanocomposite was developed to quantify vitamin B12 through cobalt(II) ion measurement. The material was derived from carbon dots capped with silver nanoparticles, forming spherical AgNPs@CDs composite with the average diameter of 8.7 nm, expressing unique optical properties, and was used as an optical probe for Co(II) ion determination. The synthesized AgNPs@CDs showed a maximum absorption peak at the wavelength of 406 nm, while their fluorescence peak appeared at the wavelength of 526 nm under the excitation wavelength of 406 nm. The nanocomposites exhibited stable fluorescence with IC50 value against human cholangiocyte cell line of 137.7 ppm after 24 h of the treatment. The interaction between Co(II) ions and AgNPs@CDs effervesced molecular oxygen and caused fluorescence quenching of the composite material. The optimum conditions for Co(II) ion detection were pH 10, volume ratio between AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ion of 0.10:0.90, and reaction time of 30 min. The linearity range based on the Stern–Volmer plot was 0.05–10.00 ppm, and the limit of detection and limit of quantification were 0.032 ppm and 0.095 ppm, respectively. The developed approach was used to determine the extracted Co(II) ions in commercial vitamin B12 supplement samples and their spiked extracts. The results showed good agreement with those from a conventional method of atomic emission spectrometry, and acceptable range of recovery from 90.0 to 105.0 % was obtained. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the AgNPs@CDs was elucidated based on the characterization information from several analytical techniques, and electrocatalytic reaction between the nanocomposite and Co(II) species was proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin B12, also known as cobalamin, is an essential micronutrient for many living organisms. It plays a crucial role in DNA and red blood cell synthesis and is important for the function of the brain and nervous system, and cellular energy production [1,2]. Despite these significant roles, mammals, including humans, cannot synthesize vitamin B12 and therefore it has to be obtained through food rich in vitamin B12 such as meat, poultry, fish, eggs, milk, and other dairy products [3]. Deficiency of vitamin B12 is commonly found worldwide, mainly owing to the low consumption of animal foods or intestinal malfunction in vitamin adsorption [4]. The risk of undernutrition from poor dietary intake of vitamin B12 can be prevented by supplementation. Determination of vitamin B12 content in supplements is necessary for consumers to consume enough and producer to quality control the products. There are four main forms of vitamin B12 depending on the R group in the structure, which are methyl-, hydroxo-, adenosyl-, and cyano-cobalamin. Among these variances, the cyano-cobalamin form is the most stable [5] and is generally included in vitamin B12 supplements for the fortification purposes.

The most commonly used techniques for vitamin B12 analysis in foods involve microbiological assay [6,7]. However, they are time-consuming and require high laboratory skills. Several analytical methods have been developed to reduce the operation time such as liquid chromatography with UV detection [8], electrochemistry [9], spectrophotometry [10], mass spectrometry [11], and chemiluminescence [12]. Analytical methods have been pioneered to quantify cobalt because compounds in the cobalamin family are cobalt complexes containing a corrin system, which directly correlates to the vitamin B12 content. These methods include flame atomic absorption spectrometry [13], electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry [14], inductively-coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [15], chemosensor [16], and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [17]. Although many analytical approaches for vitamin B12 quantification were developed, a simple, cheap, fast, selective, and sensitive method for food and supplement analysis is still lacking. Among different analytical techniques, those based on optical properties such as surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and fluorescence [18] are a rapidly growing area of research. They have been intensively exploited in various fields including environmental monitoring, industrial applications, medical research, agriculture, and food analysis, because they have the potential for miniaturization and to be developed into a test kit [19].

Nanomaterials possess unique optical properties and therefore is a suitable candidate for optical sensor development [20,21]. Metallic nanoparticles especially silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have drawn great attention because of their unique optical and physico-chemical properties, which are ideal characteristics of colorimetric sensors [22,23]. In the field of optical sensor, carbon dots (CDs)-based nanomaterials have taken the spotlight in the past two decades owing to their intriguing optical and electronic properties [24,25]. They also offered several advantages including tunable fluorescence, low toxicity, good water solubility, and facile synthesis, allowing them to grow rapidly as fluorescent sensing probes [26–28]. Due to the outstanding optical properties of AgNPs and CDs, many lines of studies further enhanced the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensing system by combining colorimetric and fluorescent probes as a composite material [29–31]. CDs were utilized as structural scaffolds for the deposition of nanostructures of transition metals to form metal–carbon functional nanocomposites [32]. Apart from their interesting optical characteristics, nitrogen-doped CDs could improve conductivity and electrochemical properties of materials [33]. The nanocomposites of AgNPs and carbon materials also exhibited electrocatalytic activity and stability [34]. Thus, they were used in the fabrication of non-enzymatic biosensors and electrochemical sensors [34–36]. Several AgNPs and CDs composite-based detection probes for heavy metal ions have been developed by exploiting the aggregation or dispersion of the nanoparticles rather than electrocatalytic activity [37–39]. However, the detection probes for cobalt ion are still yet to be developed.

This study aimed to quantify vitamin B12 in supplement samples through the detection of Co(II) ions using fluorescent probe of AgNPs@CDs. The detection method took advantage of the presence of Co(II) in vitamin B12, which had never been reported prior to this study. The developed approach offered high sensitivity and selectivity because the fluorescence response of the AgNPs@CDs was highly specific to Co(II) ions. The fluorescence stability of the optical probe and the cytotoxicity were also assessed. Optimal conditions for the detection of Co(II) ions were evaluated, and suitable extraction conditions for Co(II) ions from supplemental matrices were also examined. The analytical performance including the limit of detection, limit of quantification, linear range, accuracy, and precision of the developed method were investigated. In addition, the mechanism of interaction between Co(II) ions and AgNPs@CDs involving electrocatalytic reaction was elucidated based on the characterization results of various analytical techniques.

Citric acid was purchased from Fisher Chemicals. Urea and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were purchased from Fluka. Silver nitrate, trisodium citrate, sodium borohydride, 30 % (w/w) hydrogen peroxide, and perchlorate salt of metal ions were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Supplements were purchased from a drug store in Bangkok, Thailand. MMNK-1, human cholangiocyte cells [40] were obtained from Japanese Collection of Research Bioresources Cell Bank (Osaka, Japan).

The synthesizing procedure of CDs was modified from Kasprzyk et al. [41]. Briefly, 0.77 g of citric acid and 2.60 g of urea were dissolved in 20.0 mL of deionized water (DI). The mixture was sonicated for 5 min, and then heated under microwave-assisted irradiation (CEM MARS 6, USA) at 300 W and 140 °C for 3 min. The resulting black solid after heating was washed with three successive portions of 50.0 mL of 80 % (v/v) ethanol to remove impurities. The remnants were dispersed in ethanol and then sonicated for 5 min [42]. The precipitate consisting of large particles was separated by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was collected and kept for further use at 4 °C.

Silver nitrate of approximately 0.04 g was dissolved in 100.0 mL of deionized (DI) water. PVP of 1.40 g and trisodium citrate of 0.20 g were added to the solution with continuously stirring until well mixed, and 4 mL of hydrogen peroxide was added dropwise. Two milliliters of the synthesized CDs were added, and the mixture was heated at 65 °C for 2 h while 3 mL of 0.78 mol/L sodium borohydride were added dropwise and the mixture vigorously stirred. A clear yellow solution of the AgNPs@CDs was obtained and kept for further use at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the AgNPs@CDs and investigation on reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of the CDs and AgNPs@CDs solution were recorded using a spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453 UV–visible spectrophotometer with micro-volume cuvette) and their excitation and fluorescence emission spectra were acquired using a fluorometer (Shimadza RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer). Their fluorescence lifetimes were determined using time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system of FS5 Spectrofluorometer (EDINBURGH INSTRUMENTS). The particle sizes of CDs and AgNPs@CDs with their lattice spacing were characterized using transmission electron microscopy high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 20, 200 kV TEM/STEM/EDX/Tomography).

The functional groups of the AgNPs@CDs and their signal changes after the Co2+ ion addition were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR) (Spectrum GX FTIR System, PerkinElmer, USA) using the attenuated reflection mode and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). FTIR spectra were obtained in the range of wavenumbers from 500 to 4000 cm−1 from 64 scans with a 2 cm−1 resolution, while XPS spectra were recorded by the AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). C 1 s peak was used as an inner standard calibration peak at 285.0 eV. X-ray absorption spectrometry (XAS) was used to measure the electronic and atomic local structures of cobalt using 1.2 GeV storage ring with the beam current of 50–150 mA via Ge (220) double-crystal monochromator at Beamline 8, Synchrotron Light Research Institute (SLRI), Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum conditions and analytical performance for cobalt(II) ion detection using the AgNPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The conditions of several parameters affecting the detection efficiency of Co(II) ions were evaluated including pH of Co(II) ions in the buffer solution ranging from pH 3–12, volume ratio between AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ion solution (in a total volume of 500 μL), and reaction time ranging from 0 to 60 min.

Several factors affecting fluorescence stability of the AgNPs@CDs including irradiation time, ionic strength, temperature, and storage time were determined. A xenon (Xe) lamp (450 W) was used to expose the AgNPs@CDs, and their fluorescence intensity was measured every 20 min for 120 min of exposure for the irradiation time study. The AgNPs@CDs solutions were mixed with different concentrations of NaCl solution ranging from 0.0 to 1.0 mol/L for ionic the strength evaluation. Thermal stability of the AgNPs@CDs was conducted by heating the AgNPs@CDs solutions from 27 to 100 °C. Finally, the effects of storage time was determined as part of the investigation of long-term stability of the AgNPs@CDs by storing them at 4 °C and room temperature for 60 days.

Human cholangiocyte cell line MMNK-1 was cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) low glucose medium, supplemented with 5 % FBS and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin and maintained at 37 °C under a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator. For the cytotoxicity assay, the cells were seeded at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well in a 96-well plate. After 24 h, cells were treated with various concentrations of the AgNPs@CDs in DI water for 24 h. Cells were incubated with 0.5 % MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylth iazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) solution for 4 h. Then, the culture medium was removed and DMSO was added to solubilize the forming crystals before measurement at a wavelength of 540 nm by Multiskan™ GO Microplate Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction procedure of cobalt(II) ions from supplement samples
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for Co2+ ion extraction from vitamin B12 samples was modified from Tekin et al. [13]. Briefly, approximately 0.30 g of vitamin B12 tablet was weighed and ground. The sample powder was then dissolved in 25.0 mL of DI water and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 5 min. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove non-soluble residue, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtered solution was mixed with 65 % HNO3 (aq) at the ratio of 1:1 by volume to release cobalt ions from the complex molecule of vitamin B12. Two milliliters of the acidified extract solution was adjusted to the optimal pH using 1.0 M HCl (aq) and 1.0 M NaOH (aq). The solution with desired pH was adjusted to the final volume of 50.0 mL prior to the detection using AgNPs@CDs.

Under an optimal condition, the performance of the AgNPs@CDs for the detection of Co(II) ions was evaluated including selectivity, linearity, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), precision, and accuracy. The reliability of the developed fluorescence method was validated by measuring the recovery of the standard Co(II) ions spiked in vitamin B12 extract samples and comparing to those obtained by the conventional method of inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) (Perkin Elmer Avio 200). Significant differences between the two groups were identified using a paired sample t-test. All analyses were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of optical and physical properties of the synthesized AgNPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectrum of AgNPs@CDs showed a strong absorbance at the wavelength lower than 300 nm and the other two peaks at 330 nm and 406 nm, which is similar to absorption profile of the CDs derived from citric acid and urea with slightly less absorption wavelength (Fig. 1 A). The absorption peak at 255 nm of CDs corresponded to π–π* transition of the C=C bonds, and that at 355 nm was from n–π* transition of the C=O bonds on the surface of the CDs, while the signals at 275 and 415 nm indicated the presence of molecular formation on the surface of the CDs [41]. Since spherical AgNPs typically exhibit surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak ranging from 400 to 500 nm [44], the synthesized AgNPs@CDs showed a characteristic SPR peak for AgNPs at the wavelength of 406 nm. The results indicated the formation of AgNPs with fluorophore molecules of the CDs. The fluorescence emission peak of CDs was present at 538 nm with the excitation wavelength of 410 nm, while the emission peak of AgNPs@CDs appeared at the wavelength of 526 nm with the excitation wavelength of 406 nm. The formation of AgNPs covered the functional groups of fluorophore on the surface of CDs, resulting in the blue shift of fluorescence emission. Fluorescence lifetime is another optical characteristic influenced by the fluorophore microenvironment. To investigate interaction between the CDs and AgNPs in the composite materials, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs and AgNPs@CDs were measured, and presented as fluorescence decay profiles in Fig. 1B. The average fluorescence lifetime of the composite was 5.44 ns, which is slightly less than that of the CDs at 5.58 ns. The decrease in fluorescence lifetime could result from energy transfer process from CDs to the AgNPs.

The size and morphology of the CDs and AgNPs@CDs were characterized by the TEM and HR-TEM analyses. The images of TEM as shown in Fig. 2 A-2B indicate that both CDs and AgNPs@CDs were uniform with the average diameters of 1.2 nm and 8.7 nm, respectively. The AgNPs@CDs were spherical with the visible dark spot of CDs covered by AgNPs. The HR-TEM image of the AgNPs@CDs (Fig. 2B, inset) shows inter-planar spacing for lattice fringes of 0.228 nm and 0.198 nm, which correspond to the (111) and (200) lattice planes of Ag, respectively [31]. Moreover, the boundary line between AgNPs and CDs revealed the formation of AgNPs on the surface of CDs at which the lattice spacing of 0.201 nm corresponded to both the (103) diffraction plane of diamond-like (sp3) carbon and the (102) lattice of graphitic (sp2) carbon [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for Cobalt(II) ion determination using AgNPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the maximum sensitivity of AgNPs@CDs detection of Co(II) ions, parameters including pH of Co(II) ion solution, volume ratio of AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ion solution, and reaction time were optimized. It was found that Co(II) ions caused fluorescence quenching of the AgNPs@CDs. Therefore, detection signals were presented as relative quenching compared to blank AgNPs@CDs solution, F0/F – 1, where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of blank AgNPs@CDs and the AgNPs@CDs mixed with Co(II) ions, when the emission wavelength of 526.0 nm was measured at the excitation wavelength of 406.0 nm.

The influence of pH in the range of 3–12 on Co(II) ion detection was evaluated. Mixing Co(II) with AgNPs@CDs generated oxygen gas bubbles and the amount of bubbles increased with increasing concentration of Co(II) ions, causing the reduction of fluorescence intensity of the nanomaterials. Degrees of fluorescence quenching at different pH values of standard Co(II) ions in the buffer solution compared to DI water were measured (Fig. 3 A). Fluorescence quenching increased as pH increased from 3 to 10, at which maximum quenching was obtained. At pH higher than 10, the fluorescence quenching was reduced possibly from the precipitation of Co ions as Co(OH)2 [45,46], while AgNPs@CDs were stable over the experimental pH range.

To obtain the maximum change in fluorescence intensity, the volume ratio of AgNPs@CDs:Co(II) ions was optimized. As shown in Fig. 3B, the level of fluorescence quenching gradually increased when the ratio of AgNPs@CDs increased until reaching the maximum quenching ratio of 0.10:0.90. After this point, the quenching level sequentially decreased upon increasing AgNPs@CDs. This was possibly due to the excessive amount of fluorescent AgNPs@CDs that reacted with Co(II) ions, causing the reduction of fluorescence quenching compared to the blank solution.

The reaction time between Co(II) ions and AgNPs@CDs solution was investigated. The fluorescence quenching of the mixture increased over time. The signal of quenching increased rapidly at the beginning of the reaction because of the high concentration of free analyte ions and the nanocomposites available in the solution [47]. The reaction response was unchanged after 30 min as shown in Fig. 3C. This indicated that the reaction was completed within 30 min, and thus was chosen as the optimal reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent stability of the prepared AgNPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their application as an optical probe, fluorescent stability under several parameters including irradiation time, ionic strength, temperature, and storage time of the AgNPs@CDs was evaluated. The photostability of AgNPs@CDs was monitored by continuously irradiating with Xe lamp producing broad-spectrum wavelength for 120 min. Fluorescence intensity of the AgNPs@CDs slightly decreased after the exposure to Xe lamp for the first twenty minutes, and then the intensity were steady at higher than 92 % of their original fluorescence intensity as shown in Fig. 4 A. This indicated high photostability of the synthesized AgNPs@CDs.

The influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence emission of the AgNPs@CDs was also investigated. Different concentrations of NaCl solution ranging from 0.0 to 1.0 M were applied to the AgNPs@CDs, and the corresponding fluorescence intensities were measured as shown in Fig. 4B. The signal intensity remained constant, indicating high stability of the nanocomposites in the environment with a high ionic concentration. The stability of AgNPs@CDs under a range of thermal condition from ambient temperature (27 °C) to 100 °C was determined. The fluorescence intensity was recorded as shown in Fig. 4C. The fluorescence intensity of AgNPs@CDs remained constant between 27 °C to 70 °C, and slightly decreased after the temperature further increased from 80 °C to 100 °C. This possibly resulted from the destruction of AgNPs@CDs structures leading to the dissociation of AgNPs passivating on the nanocomposite surfaces. Effects of storage time up to 60 days at 4 °C and ambient temperature on the fluorescence stability were monitored. Fluorescence intensities of the AgNPs@CDs under different periods of storage under two different temperatures were measured. As shown in Fig. 4D, there was no significant difference in fluorescence stability between 4 °C and ambient temperature for 60 days of storage. The fluorescence intensity of AgNPs@CDs was constant for 45 days, and slightly decreased to approximately 93 % of the freshly prepared nanocomposites after 45 days. The results suggested that the AgNPs@CDs has long term stability of up to 2 months under ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity of the AgNPs@CDs to human cell line
ce:section.ce:para: Toxicity of the prepared AgNPs@CDs was evaluated against human cholangiocyte cell line MMNK-1 via MTT assay. The cells were treated with the AgNPs@CDs in the concentration range from 13.625 to 250.00 ppm for 24 h (Fig. 5 ). The cytotoxicity study revealed that the cell viability against AgNPs@CDs was dose-dependent. The IC50 value of the AgNPs@CDs to the cell after 24 h of treatment was 140.9 ppm. The IC50 values were considerably higher than the concentration of AgNPs@CDs used for Co(II) detection at approximately 40 ppm. This indicated that the AgNPs@CDs is safe for the application as a sensor probe for the Co(II) detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of AgNPs@CDs fluorescence on Cobalt(II) ion determination
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the AgNPs@CDs under the optimum conditions, the effects of several heavy metal ions of perchlorate salt, anions of sodium salts, and bioactive compounds including thiamine, riboflavin, choline, and folic acid, which are generally found as ingredients in vitamin B12 supplement, on the fluorescence signals were investigated. The results showed that AgNPs@CDs were highly selective to Co(II) ions as most of the studied metal ions did not cause significant changes in fluorescence intensity (<5%), and only a few ions could quench fluorescence emission up to 20 % compared to Co(II) ions as shown in Fig. 6 A.

The analytical figures of merit for the determination of Co(II) ions were determined. A correlation between the Co(II) ion concentration and fluorescence quenching signal was constructed following the Stern–Volmer equation presented in Eq. (1):
(1)
F 0
F
- 1 =
K
SV
Q
where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of blank AgNPs@CDs and the AgNPs@CDs mixed with Co(II) ions at different concentrations, respectively; [Q] is the quencher (Co(II) ion) concentration, and KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant.

The linearity of calibration curve covered the range of 0.05–10.00 ppm as shown in Fig. 6B. The change in physical appearance of the AgNPs@CDs was visible in the presence of Co(II) ions in which gas bubbles were developed and increased with higher concentration of Co(II) ions (Fig. 6C). LOD and LOQ were mathematically calculated based on the standard deviation (SD) of the fluorescence signal of blank solution of the AgNPs@CDs and the slope of the calibration curve. LOD and LOQ were 0.032 ppm and 0.095 ppm, respectively. The precisions of detection, expressed as relative standard deviation (% RSD) of the standard Co(II) ions at the concentrations of 0.10 ppm and 10.00 ppm, were determined to be 4.52 and 2.86, respectively.

The fluorescence method using AgNPs@CDs as a detection probe for vitamin B12 through Co(II) determination was compared to other methods previously reported for its detection performance (Table 1 ). The developed method showed several advantages over other methods including less chemical consumption, simplicity, low cost, and lower toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cobalt(II) ions in commercial nutritional supplement samples and method validation
ce:section.ce:para: The AgNPs@CDs were mixed with extracted Co(II) ions extracted from supplement samples under the optimal conditions. We found that approximately 65 % of expected Co(II) ion was extracted for a supplement containing high level of calcium. Therefore, we concluded that calcium possibly interfered with Co(II) ion detection. The tolerant limit of Ca(II) ions on Co(II) ion detection was 450 ppm of Ca(II) ions causing less than 5 % error of standard Co(II) ion detection based on the fluorescence of the AgNPs@CDs. X-ray fluorescence analysis was used to investigate the elemental composition of the vitamin B12 sample, and we found that a large amount of calcium was measured (Fig. S1). Therefore, to overcome an interference from Ca(II) ions and other possible interferences in the sample matrix causing inaccuracy in Co(II) ion determination, standard addition method following the procedure described in Supplementary Material was applied to determine Co(II) ion in the extract. It can correct the matrix effect because exactly the same sample matrix is present in both calibration standards and the sample itself.

The developed method was validated to evaluate its practicality by applying the AgNPs@CDs to extracted Co(II) ions from nutritional supplement samples. Concentrations of Co(II) ions extracted from three different supplement samples and the percent recovery of spiked samples with 0.1 and 5.0 ppm of Co(II) ion solution were determined using the standard addition method. The results were compared to those of the conventional ICP-OES technique, and the detection results were shown in Table 2 . We found that the samples of supplement number 1, 2, and 3 contained 0.11 ± 0.05, 0.15 ± 0.05, and 0.16 ± 0.08 ppm of Co ions, respectively. The results were in good agreement with those from ICP-OES (95 % CI, p < 0.05). In addition, they were also close to Co ion concentrations calculated from vitamin B12 content indicated in product labels. The recovery of the spiked samples ranged from 90.0 to 105.0 %, which was within an acceptable range. Therefore, these findings demonstrated that the developed fluorometric method using the AgNPs@CDs is an effective method for the detection of Co(II) ions extracted from commercial vitamin B12 samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between AgNPs@CDs and cobalt(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: By using a glowing splint test, the gas developed during the reaction was identified as oxygen, which acted as an efficient quencher of fluorescence. Due to the paramagnetic property, molecular oxygen promotes intersystem crossing of the excited molecules, causing the non-radiation transition to return to the ground state [52]. Therefore, the mechanism of molecular oxygen production from a reaction between AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ions was key to reveal the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. Several analytical techniques were used to elucidate the mechanism of oxygen production.

Functional groups on the surface of AgNPs@CDs and their signal changes after reacting with Co(II) ions was determined using FTIR. The signals of AgNPs@CDs showed a broad peak ranging from 3,000–3,500 cm−1 which was attributed to the O-H and N-H stretching vibration. The peak at around 2,900 cm−1 corresponded to C-H stretching. A strong peak at 1,649.3 cm−1 belonged to C=O stretching of the amide group in the PVP molecule. The peaks at 1,421.5 cm−1 and 1,375.2 cm−1 were associated with CH2 scissoring and C-H bending, respectively. In addition, the signal of C-N stretching was observed at 1,287.4 cm−1. The peaks at 1,074.0 cm−1 for C-O stretching and at 843.1 cm−1 for C-C stretching were identified (Fig. S2) [53,54]. After the Co(II) addition, most of the characteristic FTIR peaks were not significantly changed. Only the signal of O-H and N-H stretching became broader, which indicated an interaction between the functional groups and Co(II) ions.

To gain an insight into the interaction between AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ions, an XPS analysis was used to identify the chemical compositions and chemical states of the materials. The results revealed that AgNPs@CDs were composed of elements including C, O, N, and Ag (Fig. 7 A). The high resolution spectrum of binding energy (B.E.) for C 1s showed four peaks at 284.652, 285.680, 287.208, and 288.581 eV (Fig. S3A), which corresponded to carbon atom in C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, C=O/C=N, and HO-C=O, respectively. The binding energies of O 1s showed the signals at 530.700, 531.794, and 532.899 eV (Fig. 7B), which can be assigned to oxygen atom in the group of N-(C=O), C=O, and C-O-H, respectively. The binding energy of N 1s comprised two peaks at 399.344 and 400.470 eV (Fig. 7C), which corresponded to N-(C=O) and C-N-H, respectively. In addition, the formation of AgNPs could be confirmed by the presence of two peaks of binding energy for Ag 3d at 368.269 and 374.261 eV (Fig. S3B), belonging to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively. The results obtained from XPS were consistent with those from FTIR. Therefore, we concluded that functional groups of the AgNPs@CDs mainly consisted of amide, amino, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups [55].

The elemental compositions of the materials after adding Co(II) ions did not change significantly. The signal of Co atom could not be detected, which may be due to the low concentration of Co ion added. The binding energy shift was measured and we found that the four peaks of C 1s and 2 peaks of Ag 3d spectra were similar to that of AgNPs@CDs prior to Co(II) ion addition (Fig. S3C-S3D). This suggested that carbon and silver were not an active site involved in the electron transfer between AgNPs@CDs and Co(II) ions. However, the binding energies of O 1s and N 1s changed significantly, of which the energy differences were more than 0.5 eV. The binding energies of O 1s for all functional groups detected including N-(C=O), C=O, and C-O-H significantly increased (Fig. 7D), indicating that these oxygen containing groups acted as electron donating groups to Co(II) ions. For N 1s, the peak of C-N-H disappeared. Hence, only signal of N-(C=O) was detected after the Co(II) addition (Fig. 7E). The disappearance of C-N-H signal possibly resulted from the oxidation of carbon atom that became a carbonyl in the amine molecule. Alternatively, C-N-H could act as an electron acceptor group from electron rich species causing an increase in binding energy that overlapped with the signal of N-(C=O).

XAS analysis was performed to determine the valence state of cobalt present in AgNPs@CDs environment because the cobalt signal could not be detected. X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) was used to elucidate the local electronic structure of cobalt involved in the oxygen-evolving reaction. The XANES spectra of Co K-edge of Co(II) ions with AgNPs@CDs and Co(ClO4)2·6H2O, a reference compound, had the edge energies of 7720.03 and 7719.88 eV, which were not significantly different (Fig. 7F). This indicated that the electronic state of Co(II) ions remained unchanged during the process of molecular oxygen evolution.

Based on the characterization results, we concluded that the surface of AgNPs@CDs mainly consisted of amide, amino, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups. Oxygen containing functionalities including amide, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups acted as electron donating groups to form complex with Co(II) ions, while nitrogen atoms in the amino group accepted electrons from electron rich species. There was no change in the electronic state in either AgNPs capped CDs or Co(II) ions, indicating that they were not involved in a redox reaction for hydrolysis to generate molecular oxygen in an aqueous solution. Interestingly, we found that the mechanism of oxygen gas evolution was possibly explained by the previous report by Zhang et al. They found that silver nanoparticles coupled to Co(OH)2 nanosheet grown on carbon cloth exhibited an excellent catalytic activity and stability for catalyzing spontaneous oxygen evolution in alkaline media [56]. Charge transfer from Co(OH)2 to Ag cluster occurred, resulting in an electron-deficient region on Co(OH)2 and electron-rich region on Ag. Under an alkaline condition, OH– ion attached to the active site on the Ag cluster to form the structure of
OH intermediates absorbed on Ag@Co(OH)2 through the oxidation process followed by
O and
OOH, respectively. Finally,
OOH reacted with OH– to produce an oxygen molecule and water with Ag@Co(OH)2 as electrocatalyst. Therefore, the proposed mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the AgNPs@CDs by Co(II) ions is as depicted in Scheme 1 . Co(OH)2 was formed in an alkaline solution, and adsorbed and stabilized on the particles surface of AgNPs@CDs through interacting with their functional groups. The nanoparticle complex then acted as an electrocatalyst for the oxygen gas evolution, which caused fluorescence quenching of the AgNPs@CDs.

This work presents the application of the nanocomposite as a detection sensor by harnessing the optical and electrocatalytic properties. We developed a facile method of preparing AgNPs capped CDs nanocomposite for Co(II) ion quantification. Oxygen gas was generated when Co(II) ions were in an alkaline medium of the nanocomposite materials. The amount of oxygen evolved was positively correlated to the degree of fluorescence quenching. The mechanism of molecular oxygen evolution was proposed to be an electrocatalytic reaction by the AgNPs capped onto CDs and Co(OH)2. The fluorescence quenching approach was successfully used to measure Co(II) ions extracted from vitamin B12 supplement samples for vitamin B12 assessment. The approach offered several advantages including simplicity, cost effectiveness, high throughput, high selectivity, and high sensitivity under optimal conditions. In addition, toxicity study against Human cholangiocyte cell line MMNK-1 used in the application of Co(II) detection showed that AgNPs@CDs were non-toxic. The measurement results from the proposed method were consistent with those of standard analytical method (95 % CI, p < 0.05). This demonstrated the reliability of the developed fluorescence quenching method, which can be used as an effective alternative for the determination of Co(II) ion-containing samples.

Ratchadaporn Seedad: Methodology, Investigation. Pornpimol Prayongpan: Methodology, Investigation. Kamonchanok Chualong: Methodology, Investigation. Chanpen Karuwan: Validation, Formal analysis. Kriangsak Songsrirote: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

I would like to thank Dr. Wutthikrai Busayaporn for assistant in XAS conceptual discussions. Assistance provided from beamline 8 (BL8) staff members of the synchrotron facility is also gratefully acknowledged. In addition, I would like to thank Assoc. Prof. Arthit Chairoungdua from Department of Physiology, Faculty of Science, Mahidol University and Assist. Prof. Nuttapon Apiratikul from Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Srinakharinwirot University for their kind support on cytotoxic experiment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110595.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
